title: NIS2 Network and Information Systems Access Control Audit

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing: SPL directly addresses Art.21(2)(i) requirements with specific, actionable monitoring.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(i) (Human resources and access control) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.5: NIS2 Network and Information Systems Access Control Audit.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.2.5' running on index=windows sourcetype="WinEventLog:Security" (EventCode, Account_Name, Logon_Type, Workstation_Name, Status, dest), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

spl:
index=windows sourcetype="WinEventLog:Security" EventCode IN (4624, 4625, 4672) earliest=-24h
| eval auth_result=case(EventCode=4624,"success", EventCode=4625,"failure", EventCode=4672,"special_privileges", 1=1,"other")
| eval after_hours=if(tonumber(strftime(_time,"%H"))<7 OR tonumber(strftime(_time,"%H"))>19, 1, 0)
| stats count by EventCode, auth_result, Account_Name, dest, Logon_Type, after_hours
| sort -count
description: Audits interactive logon success/failure and special privilege assignment on Windows assets supporting essential services, including after-hours and non-interactive patterns, for access-control assurance.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for NIS2 compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes windows and sourcetypes WinEventLog:Security. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Deploy Splunk Add-on for Windows (742) with Security log collection from domain controllers and member servers; (2) enable Group Policy auditing for logon events and special privileges; (3) tune out known service accounts via `lookup service_accounts.csv`; (4) send high-value rows (4625 spikes, 4672 after-hours) to SOAR/ITSM; (5) map to Authentication CIM for ES content. Indexes required: `index=windows`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security`.
visualization: Time chart (failed logons 4625), Table (privileged logons 4672), Bar chart (after_hours vs business hours).
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.user, Authentication.action, Authentication.src | sort - count
knownFalsePositives: Operational noise during large maintenance windows, controlled exercises, or scope changes to critical entity lists.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase 742).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=windows` `sourcetype="WinEventLog:Security"` (EventCode, Account_Name, Logon_Type, Workstation_Name, Status, dest).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Deploy Splunk Add-on for Windows (742) with Security log collection from domain controllers and member servers; (2) enable Group Policy auditing for logon events and special privileges; (3) tune out known service accounts via `lookup service_accounts.csv`; (4) send high-value rows (4625 spikes, 4672 after-hours) to SOAR/ITSM; (5) map to Authentication CIM for ES content.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=windows sourcetype="WinEventLog:Security" EventCode IN (4624, 4625, 4672) earliest=-24h
| eval auth_result=case(EventCode=4624,"success", EventCode=4625,"failure", EventCode=4672,"special_privileges", 1=1,"other")
| eval after_hours=if(tonumber(strftime(_time,"%H"))<7 OR tonumber(strftime(_time,"%H"))>19, 1, 0)
| stats count by EventCode, auth_result, Account_Name, dest, Logon_Type, after_hours
| sort -count
```

Understanding this SPL

**NIS2 Network and Information Systems Access Control Audit (Art. 21(2)(i))** — Audits interactive logon success/failure and special privilege assignment on Windows assets supporting essential services, including after-hours and non-interactive patterns, for access-control assurance.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=windows` `sourcetype="WinEventLog:Security"` (EventCode, Account_Name, Logon_Type, Workstation_Name, Status, dest). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase 742). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: windows; **sourcetype**: WinEventLog:Security. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=windows, sourcetype="WinEventLog:Security", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **auth_result** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **after_hours** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by EventCode, auth_result, Account_Name, dest, Logon_Type, after_hours** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

See also the **CIM SPL** field in this use case; enable the matching data model acceleration if you run the tstats variant in production.


Step 3 — Validate
Compare with audit evidence in the source system (DLP, IDP, or application) and, if this use case underpins a control test, the ServiceNow GRC console or the evidence pack for a known time window.


Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Time chart (failed logons 4625), Table (privileged logons 4672), Bar chart (after_hours vs business hours).
grandmaExplanation: We audits interactive logon success/failure and special privilege assignment on Windows assets supporting essential services, including after-hours and non-interactive patterns, for access-control assurance.
